title: Brand Identity on a Budget: Looking Like You Mean It
subtitle: Your brand is not your logo. It is the promise customers can count on every visit.

sections.heading: Brand Is Not a Logo
sections.body:
Your brand is not your logo, your colour scheme, or your Instagram filter. Those are expressions of your brand. Your brand is the answer to: "What does this cafe stand for, and why should I choose it over the one next door?"

A strong brand for a cafe means:
- Customers can describe you in one sentence ("It's the cosy one with amazing pastries")
- The experience is consistent across every touchpoint (signage, menu, social media, service)
- The team understands and embodies the brand without being told
- You attract the right customers — the ones who value what you offer

A weak brand means:
- Customers cannot remember your name
- The signage says one thing, the menu says another, and Instagram says a third
- Staff are not sure what makes the cafe different from any other
- You compete on price because you have nothing else to compete on

You do not need a branding agency. You need clarity about who you are and the discipline to express it consistently.

sections.heading: The One-Page Style Guide
sections.body:
Use T140-01 (Brand Style Guide Template):

A style guide ensures consistency. It answers: when we create anything (a menu, a social post, a sign, a cup), what should it look and feel like?

**Your one-page style guide should include:**

**Brand statement:** One sentence. "[Cafe name] is a specialty coffee shop and neighbourhood gathering place, serving craft coffee and honest food in a warm, welcoming space."

**Tone of voice:** How do you sound? Warm and informal? Professional and precise? Playful and cheeky? Give 3–4 adjective pairs:
- Warm but not cheesy
- Knowledgeable but not pretentious
- Casual but not sloppy

**Colours:** 2–3 colours maximum. One primary (your dominant colour), one secondary (accent), one neutral (text/background). Specify hex codes so they are consistent across print and digital.

**Fonts:** One for headings, one for body text. Simple and readable. If you do not have brand fonts, choose from free options (Google Fonts has excellent choices).

**Photography style:** Natural light, warm tones, real products (not stock), focus on texture and detail. Include 3–4 example photos that represent the standard.

**Logo usage:** Where the logo appears, minimum size, clear space around it, what backgrounds it works on.

Print this page. Give it to anyone who creates content for your cafe — social media, menu design, signage, business cards. Consistency is the visible expression of professionalism.

sections.heading: Brand Consistency Across Touchpoints
sections.body:
Use T142-01 (Brand Touchpoint Consistency Check):

Every interaction a customer has with your cafe is a touchpoint. Each one should feel like it comes from the same place.

**Audit these touchpoints quarterly:**

| Touchpoint | Consistent? | Notes |
|-----------|------------|-------|
| Exterior signage | | |
| A-frame / pavement sign | | |
| Window display | | |
| Interior design | | |
| Menu board / printed menu | | |
| Takeaway cups and packaging | | |
| Till receipts | | |
| Staff appearance / aprons | | |
| Website | | |
| Instagram | | |
| Google Business Profile | | |
| Review responses | | |
| Business cards | | |
| Email signature | | |
| Catering materials | | |

Look for mismatches. A beautiful Instagram feed paired with a poorly designed menu board. A warm website paired with generic takeaway cups. A brand that says "specialty" paired with sugar packets from a cash-and-carry.

You do not need to fix everything at once. Prioritise the touchpoints that the most customers see: exterior, menu, cups, and social media. Upgrade one per quarter.

Branded cups are one of the highest-impact investments. Your cup walks through the city on someone's hand — it is a mobile advertisement. Custom-printed cups cost more than generic ones, but the marketing value per cup is hard to beat.

sections.heading: New Customer Acquisition
sections.body:
Use T141-01 (Customer Acquisition Channels Audit):

Most cafes rely on foot traffic and word of mouth. These are powerful channels — but they are passive. Active acquisition means deliberately bringing new customers through your door.

**Channels to evaluate:**

| Channel | Current Effort | Potential | Cost |
|---------|---------------|-----------|------|
| Walk-by foot traffic | Exterior, signage, A-frame | High | Low |
| Google search / Maps | Google Business Profile | High | Free |
| Instagram | Regular posting, local hashtags | Medium | Free |
| Word of mouth | Quality service, memorable experience | High | Free |
| Reviews | Review generation programme | High | Free |
| Local partnerships | Cross-promotion with neighbours | Medium | Free |
| Events | Hosting or attending local events | Medium | Low |
| Flyers / local advertising | Leaflets in local businesses | Low | Low |
| Delivery platforms | Deliveroo, UberEats exposure | Medium | High |
| PR / press | Local food blogs, newspaper features | High | Free |

**The highest-ROI acquisition channels for cafes:**
1. Google Business Profile (free, high-intent searchers)
2. Word of mouth from delighted customers (free, highest trust)
3. Instagram with local hashtags and location tags (free, visual)
4. Local partnerships and cross-promotion (free, community)

Focus on making these four excellent before spending money on advertising. Paid channels (social media ads, flyers, delivery platforms) have a place, but free channels should be maxed out first.

sections.heading: Community Marketing and Local Partnerships
sections.body:
Use T143-01 (Local Partnership Tracker):

Local marketing is about being woven into the neighbourhood — not shouting at it from a billboard.

**Partnership ideas:**

**Neighbouring businesses:** Mutual promotion. Their flyer on your counter, your flyer on theirs. "Show your receipt from [bookshop] for 10% off coffee" — drives traffic both ways.

**Local offices:** Monthly coffee delivery, meeting catering, corporate discount cards. Walk in, introduce yourself, leave a menu. The first order usually leads to repeat business.

**Schools and nurseries:** Sponsor the school fair, provide coffee for parents' evening, offer a "school run" discount. Parents are a reliable, frequent customer base.

**Gyms and studios:** Post-workout coffee discount for members. Their clientele values quality food and drink. Cross-promote.

**Local creatives:** Host art exhibitions, photography shows, or poetry readings. Artists bring an audience, you provide the space. Both benefit.

**Seasonal marketing calendar (T144-01):**
Plan marketing activities around the year:
- January: New Year menu launch, "warm up" campaign
- February: Valentine's specials
- March–April: Spring menu, outdoor seating reopening
- Summer: Iced drinks programme, outdoor events
- September: Back-to-school, autumn menu
- October–December: Christmas specials, gift offerings, seasonal events

Having a calendar prevents the "I should post something" panic. When activities are planned, they happen.

sections.heading: Events, Collaborations, and PR
sections.body:
Use T144-01 (Events & PR Calendar):

**Local PR — getting noticed:**

Local food bloggers, newspaper food columnists, and Instagram food accounts are always looking for content. Make it easy for them to feature you:

1. **Identify 5–10 local food influencers and journalists.** Follow them, engage with their content, understand what they cover.
2. **Invite them.** A simple DM or email: "We'd love to have you visit [cafe name]. We've just launched [new item/concept] and think it might interest you. Coffee is on us."
3. **Make the visit special.** When they arrive, give them the best table, the best version of your products, and a brief personal introduction. They will write about the experience — make it memorable.
4. **Send a follow-up.** A thank-you message with high-resolution photos they can use. Make it easy for them to feature you.

**Collaborations that work:**

- **Guest roaster week:** Feature a different roaster for a week. Their customers visit you; your customers try something new.
- **Baker collaboration:** A local baker creates an exclusive item for your cafe. Joint promotion on both channels.
- **Brand partnership:** A local candle maker, ceramicist, or illustrator creates something branded for your cafe. Sell it in-store. Joint promotion.

**Event PR:** When you host an event, tell people about it:
- Submit to local event listings (free)
- Post on social media 2 weeks, 1 week, and 1 day before
- Share photos and highlights afterwards
- Tag partners and attendees (extends reach)

PR is not about being famous. It is about being known in your neighbourhood. When someone asks "Do you know a good cafe near here?" — your name should be the answer.
